title: Chicken tagine with olives
subtitle: Moroccan slow-braised chicken with saffron, ginger, and green olives.
excerpt: A Moroccan chicken braise with the household trio of saffron, ginger, and lemon. Bone-in thighs cook gently with onion and garlic until the meat is falling tender, then a heavy hand of green olives and chopped herbs goes in at the end. Served over couscous or with bread to mop the sauce.
sourceNotes: Tagine names both the conical earthenware pot and any of the slow stews cooked in it. Chicken tagine with olives (poulet aux olives) is one of the half-dozen canonical household preparations across Morocco; the related preserved-lemon-and-olive version (mqualli) is its more formal cousin. The spice base (saffron, ginger, turmeric, and cumin) is the broader Moroccan chicken signature; the olives are added late so they hold shape and brininess. The dish can be cooked in an earthenware tagine over low charcoal heat, on a hob with a heat diffuser, or in a heavy casserole in a low oven. The braise itself is older than the named version and predates the standardisation of Moroccan cookery in the early twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to three months. Defrost overnight and warm gently; the olives soften further but hold shape.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly across two pans, or use one very wide casserole. Do not stack the chicken in the pot; one snug layer cooks evenly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better on day two. Make the day before, refrigerate in the sauce, reheat gently.

glossaryTerms.definition: Moroccan aged salted butter, sometimes flavoured with herbs. Strong, slightly funky, used in small quantities to enrich tagines and couscous. Ghee is the practical substitute outside North Africa.

content.text: Chicken thighs on the bone, skin on. The bone gives the sauce body; the skin browns and adds fat. Boneless thighs work but the dish tastes thinner. Six thighs feed four with bread; eight feed four with couscous and have leftovers.

The spice paste is mixed in a small bowl and rubbed into the chicken before cooking. Saffron is the splurge ingredient and the colour-and-flavour signature; soak a small pinch in two tablespoons of warm water for ten minutes to release the colour. Cracked green olives (preferably picholine or any unstuffed firm green olive) go in for the last ten minutes only. A scatter of fresh coriander and a squeeze of lemon at the end lifts the dish out of pure savouriness.
What you need

items.prepNote: steeped in 2 tbsp warm water for 10 minutes
1 zested for the spice rub, 1 juiced for finishing

content.text: Method
Rub the chicken in spice

Pull a small bowl. Combine the {{turmeric}} of turmeric, the {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, the {{coriander-ground}} of coriander, the {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, the {{cinnamon-ground}} of cinnamon, the {{ginger-root}} of grated ginger, two of the {{garlic}} cloves' worth of grated garlic (keep the rest for the onions), the zest of one of the {{lemon}}s, a generous pinch of salt, and a turn of pepper. Add one tablespoon of the {{olive-oil}} to make a paste. Pat the {{chicken-thigh}} thighs dry and rub the paste into every surface. Let stand for fifteen to thirty minutes.
Brown the chicken

Heat the remaining olive oil in a heavy casserole or tagine over medium-high heat. Lay the thighs in skin-side down and cook for five to six minutes without moving, until the skin is deeply golden. Flip and cook for three minutes more. Lift onto a plate. Do not crowd; brown in two batches if needed.
Soften the onions and build the base

Lower the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} chopped onions to the same pan and cook for eight minutes, scraping up any spice fond. Stir in the remaining grated garlic and cook for thirty seconds. Pour in the {{stock-chicken}} of hot stock and the steeped saffron with its soaking water. Bring to a simmer.
Braise

Return the chicken to the pan, skin-side up, snug in one layer. The liquid should come about halfway up the thighs, not over the skin (the skin needs to stay above the liquid to keep its colour and texture). Cover and simmer over low heat for thirty minutes. The chicken should be fully cooked, the meat starting to pull from the bone, the sauce thickened slightly.
Finish with olives and herbs

Scatter the {{olives-green}} of green olives around the chicken. Cover and cook for ten minutes more. Lift the lid; if the sauce is thin, raise the heat and bubble uncovered for five minutes. Squeeze in the juice of the second {{lemon}}. Scatter the {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley and the {{coriander}} of chopped fresh coriander over the top. Serve straight from the pan, with couscous, bread, or rice.

items.cause: Spice rub not bloomed; the chicken went straight in.
items.fix: Either rest the rubbed chicken for fifteen minutes (the salt draws moisture and dissolves the spice) or toast the spices in the oil for thirty seconds before the onions go in.

items.symptom: Chicken skin is pale and rubbery
items.cause: Liquid was too high in the pan, or the lid trapped steam directly on the skin.
items.fix: Liquid should sit halfway up the thighs, not over the skin. If the lid sits very tight, prop it slightly open with a wooden spoon to let steam escape.

items.cause: Cooked too long, or were not the right kind.
items.fix: Use unstuffed cracked green olives; pitted olives also work but break down faster. Ten minutes in the sauce is enough; pulled in earlier they release too much brine.

items.cause: Stock too high, or braise too short.
items.fix: Uncover for the last five minutes and raise the heat to reduce. A small spoonful of cornflour slaked in water can rescue a stubbornly thin sauce.

items.symptom: Saffron has not coloured the sauce
items.cause: Saffron not steeped, or strands were old.
items.fix: Buy saffron in small quantities; it loses colour after a year. Steep in warm water for ten minutes; the water should turn deep orange-yellow.

content.text: Variations

Preserved lemon is the more formal version (mqualli): use half a preserved lemon, rinsed and quartered, in place of the lemon zest and juice. Apricots in place of olives makes a sweeter Berber-leaning tagine; soak 100 g of dried apricots in warm water for ten minutes first. A spoon of
smen
stirred in with the onions deepens the dish; ghee is the practical substitute.
Where this dish lives

Chicken tagine is the Moroccan household supper for a long Friday or a Sunday lunch. The conical clay pot it is named for traps and recirculates steam, so the sauce stays moist over many hours of cooking; a heavy casserole with a tight lid does roughly the same. The dish belongs to the broader Moroccan tradition of slow stews finished with one bright element: olives, preserved lemon, dried fruit, or chickpeas. The spice base holds across them all; what changes is the bright finish.
